name: First law of thermodynamics
question_text: Which statement best expresses the first law of thermodynamics?
general_feedback: The first law is the thermodynamic statement of energy conservation: the change in internal energy of a system equals the heat added to the system minus the work done by the system (ΔU = Q − W). Energy is neither created nor destroyed; it changes form.
correct_feedback: Correct — This is exactly the first law — the thermodynamic statement of energy conservation.

answers.text: Energy is conserved; it changes form but is not created or destroyed.
answers.feedback: This is exactly the first law — the thermodynamic statement of energy conservation.

answers.text: Heat always flows spontaneously from cold to hot.
answers.feedback: That contradicts the second law, which says heat flows from hot to cold without external work.

answers.text: Entropy of an isolated system always decreases.
answers.feedback: The second law states entropy of an isolated system never decreases, not the first law.

answers.text: No heat engine can convert heat entirely into work in a single cycle.
answers.feedback: That is the Kelvin statement of the second law, not the first.
